content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 307 · SACRAMENTO, 95834 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

June 28, 1976


Mr. George G. Hardie
Chairman, Citizens For
Greyhound Racing
6399 Wilshire Boulevard
Los Angeles, California 90012

Dear Mr. Hardie:

This letter answers various questions you raised
in a telephone conversation with Ms. Nan Hambleton of
the Technical Assistance and Analysis Division of the
Fair Political Practices Commission on May 26, 1976.

Question 1: Must you, as proponent of the Greyhound
Racing Initiative, attach statements to your
campaign filing showing the contributions
received and expenditures made by the Citizens
For Greyhound Racing committee and the contri-
butions received and expenditures made by all
other groups or individuals which supported
qualification of the initiative for the ballot?

Answer: Yes.

Elections Code Section 3521 1/ provides that only the
proponent of a proposition and persons authorized in writing
by the proponent may file petitions in support thereof.
Such petitions are the only ones which may be considered by
the county clerk or registrar of voters in determining
whether a measure has qualified for the ballot. Criminal
penalties attach for a failure by any individual who has
circulated petitions to turn them over to the proponent.2/

_________________________________

1/ Formerly Article IV Section 1 of the California Constitution.

2/ California Elections Code Section 29226.


Mr. George G. Hardie
Page Two

June 28, 1976

Government Code Section 84202 provides that the proponent
of a ballot measure must file a campaign statement no later
than 65 days after a measure has qualified for the ballot.
Only the proponent of such a measure is required to make such
a filing. Those who have opposed the proponent's efforts
prior to a measure's qualification have no similar filing
requirement.3/

Those sections read together indicate that the proponent
has the responsibility to disclose all information relating
to his campaign to qualify the measure for the ballot whether
such activity was engaged in directly by the proponent, or
by his agents.

Accordingly, in preparing the proponent's campaign state-
ment, information relative to contributions and expenditures
of all groups or individuals who acted in support of the
drive must be submitted in one consolidated report. This
report, however, may incorporate by reference separate sched-
ules prepared by those groups or individuals which indepen-
dently received or expended funds in furtherance of the
initiative campaign. Nevertheless, the authorized proponent
remains responsible for the accuracy of the information con-
tained in each of the consolidated or incorporated schedules.4/

_________________________________________________________________

3/ However, after qualification of a measure, all groups or
individuals involved in the campaign seeking the passage
or defeat of the measure which are committees as defined
by Government Code Section 82013 must file disclosure
statements in accordance with Government Code Section
84203. These statements are due not later than 35 days
prior to the election, not later than 7 days prior to the
election and again not later than 70 days after the election.

4/ If, as the Commission expects, the California Supreme
Court upholds Government Code Section 85200, which requires
that all expenditures relating to the circulation of a
statewide petition must be made with the express or
implied authorization of the proponent, then the above
analysis will be supported by this section as well. Sec-
tion 85200 is consistent with Elections Code Sections
3521 and 29226 in that it recognized that the proponent
controls the circulation drive.


[illegible]

Mr. George G. Hardie
Page Three                                      June 28, 1976


Question 2: Do "independent" groups or individuals which
assisted the campaign to qualify the initiative for
the ballot have an obligation to file separate cam-
paign statements?

Answer: No.

     In your conversation with Ms. Hembleton you indicated
that "a man in Oakland", acting independently from you, opened
his own bank account and spent money to qualify the measure
for the ballot. Since as the proponent you have the responsi-
bility to disclose fully each of the expenditures and contri-
butions made in furtherance of your drive, you must include
the information relating to all individuals and groups which
supported your campaign. Furthermore, as indicated above, if
this individual collected signatures, the registrar of voters
would be prohibited by the Elections Code Section 3521 from
considering or counting them unless he was authorized by you
to file them. Therefore, as proponent you must account in your
account in your campaign statement for all of his contribu-
tions and expenditures, this individual has no independent
filing requirement.5/

Question 3: Do you have an obligation to report a $5,000
contribution you personally made to the Citizens For
Greyhound Racing?

Answer: Yes.

     As proponent of the Greyhound Racing Initiative you are
required to personally disclose all contributions and expen-
ditures received by your campaign. Your $5,000 personal con-
tribution must be included in this filing. This requirement
arises out of your status as a "proponent", and it is thus


5/ However, such groups or individuals must file reports
pursuant to Government Code Section 84203 reporting their
activities in furtherance of or in opposition to the
ballot measure once it qualifies, provided they are com-
mittees as defined in Government Code Section 82013. The
dates for such filings are set forth in Section 84203.

Mr. George G. Hardie
Page Four                                    June 28, 1976


     irrelevant whether your $5,000 contribution makes you a
"major donor", i.e., a committee described in Government
Code Section 82013(c).6/

Question 4: Would a receipt for payment signed by a peti-
     tion circulator who was paid in cash constitute a
written instrument in satisfaction of Government Code
Section 84300?

     Answer: No.

     The prohibition against cash contributions or expendi-
tures set forth in Government Code Section 84300 is absolute.
No illegal cash contribution or expenditure may be legitimized
by a record of such illegal payment or receipt. Instead a
written instrument containing the name of the donor and the
name of the donee is required for any contribution of $50 or
more, and a written instrument such as a check or money order
is required when an expenditure of $50 or more is made. This
requirement is designed to insure that an independent record
of the transfer of substantial amounts of money is preserved
for later reporting, auditing and investigating purposes.

Question 5: Is it a violation of Government Code Section
     84300 to pay a solicitor in cash amounts of less than
$50 on several occasions where the aggregate amount
of such payments to this individual equals or exceeds
$50?

     Answer: No.

     Government Code Section 84300 prohibits the expenditure
of cash in amounts of $50 or more. No expenditure may be made


_____________________________

6/ I do not address the question whether you would be
     required to disclose, as a major donor, any contribu-
     tions you make to other candidates or campaigns. Nor
     do I consider whether other contributors to your cam-
     paign would be required to file major donor statements
     if they would otherwise meet the definition requiring
     such a filing set forth in Government Code Section 82013(c).

Mr. George C. Hardie
Page Five                                June 28, 1976


in cash in such a way as to avoid the prohibition on expendi-
tures of $50 or more being made in cash.  Thus an individual
expenditure equal to or exceeding $50 may not be paid in
separate installments of cash in order to avoid having a
record of the expenditure.  At the same time, however, bona
fide expenditures of less than $50 may be made in cash on
different occasions to the same recipient even if the total
amount paid circulator during the reporting period equals
or exceeds $50.  However, if a circulator has been paid amounts
of cash which have equalled or exceeded $50 through cumulation,
Government Code Section 84210(d) requires that the filer dis-
close the name and address of such a person and the total he
received during the reporting period.

Question 6:  When must a proponent file his campaign statement?

Answer:

     As pointed out above, Government Code Section 84202 pro-
vides that the filing must be made 65 days after the Secre-
tary of State's office determines that a measure has quali-
fied for the ballot.  We understand that your measure
qualified on June 9, 1976.  Accordingly, your filing is due
not later than August 13, 1976.

     If any of the answers to the foregoing questions re-
quire further explanation do not hesitate to contact me.

                                        Sincerely yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:ne

cc:  Frank Duncan, Esquire

irrelevant whether your $5,000 contribution makes you a "major donor", i.e., a committee described in Government Code Section 82013(c).6/

Answer: No.

6/ I do not address the question whether you would be required to disclose, as a major donor, any contributions you make to other candidates or campaigns. Nor do I consider whether other contributors to your campaign would be required to file major donor statements if they would otherwise meet the definition requiring such a filing set forth in Government Code Section 82013(c).

in cash in such a way as to avoid the prohibition on expenditures of $50 or more being made in cash. Thus an individual expenditure equal to or exceeding $50 may not be paid in separate installments of cash in order to avoid leaving a record of the expenditure. At the same time, however, bona fide expenditures of less than $50 may be made in cash on different occasions to the same recipient even if the total amount paid the circulator during the reporting period equals or exceeds $50. However, if a circulator has been paid amounts of cash which have equaled or exceeded $50 through cumulation, Government Code Section 84210(d) requires that the filer disclose the name and address of such a person and the total he received during the reporting period.

Question 6: When must a proponent file his campaign statement?

Answer:

As pointed out above, Government Code Section 84202 provides that the filing must be made 65 days after the Secretary of State's office determines that a measure has qualified for the ballot. We understand that your measure qualified on June 9, 1976. Accordingly, your filing is due not later than August 13, 1976.

If any of the answers to the foregoing questions require further explanation do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely yours,

[signature]
Michael Bennett
Executive Director
